name: ETH 1h Inventory Reset Reclaim Runner v1
edge_mechanism: short-term inventory gets stretched away from value, price reclaims fast value, and the trade monetizes the rotation with an early partial plus a smaller structure-managed runner
thesis: ETH 1h is the best fit lane for a dense inventory-reset continuation concept because the regime snapshot is chop-dominant with transition contamination. The edge is not a static oversold bounce. It is trading the reclaim after inventory gets offside, then letting a smaller core ride only if the reset becomes a larger directional move.
invalidation_condition: If reclaim events do not occur frequently enough, if the first partial hit rate is poor, or if reclaims repeatedly fail before price can rotate beyond costs, the mechanism fails.

pre_submit_reasoning.market_behavior_targeted: Price stretches away from short-term value, reclaims it as offside inventory exits, and then rotates back toward medium balance with occasional extension beyond that first move.
pre_submit_reasoning.why_edge_should_exist: Choppy 1h structure tends to punish late extension entries and reward reclaim-to-balance moves. ETH is active enough to produce repeated resets while remaining cleaner than AXS.
pre_submit_reasoning.asset_timeframe_fit: ETH 1h is the natural home for this mechanism because it should be much denser than 4h while still maintaining enough structure for reclaims to matter.

indicator_role_map.EMA_55: medium balance target and runner management anchor

pre_submit_reasoning.likely_failure_mode: The reclaim may happen inside a real breakout, turning the supposed reset into a bad fade against genuine directional movement.
pre_submit_reasoning.first_refinement_path: If density is good but runner expectancy is poor, cut runner share and keep more profit in the early scale-out instead of adding more filters.
pre_submit_reasoning.expected_outcome_pattern: Many first-target hits, a solid number of second-target hits, and only occasional runner contribution when the reset becomes a larger impulse.
pre_submit_reasoning.what_would_count_as_success: Screen shows credible density, strong first-target capture rate, and at least one cross-lane preserves the same reclaim structure.
pre_submit_reasoning.what_would_count_as_failure: 0 trades, sub-10 trade screen output, or repeated reclaim failures before first partial.

density_sanity_check.event_sparsity: low because the trigger is stretch-plus-reclaim rather than a ceremonial chain

validation_basket.why_included: Best-fit lane for dense inventory-reset reclaim logic.
validation_basket.continue_if: ETH 1h shows repeated reclaim rotations with positive post-cost first-target behavior.
validation_basket.rotate_away_if: ETH 1h reclaims are too shallow or too noisy to monetize.
validation_basket.expected_if_right: High enough participation to validate the mechanism, with most edge coming from early partials.

validation_basket.why_included: Checks whether the same reclaim logic survives on another liquid major with different volatility amplitude.
validation_basket.continue_if: BTC 1h preserves the same reclaim-to-balance sequence with cleaner execution.
validation_basket.rotate_away_if: BTC 1h suppresses the setup into low density.
validation_basket.expected_if_right: Lower count than ETH but cleaner reclaims.

validation_basket.why_included: Tests whether the same mechanism survives slower structure without collapsing into sparse behavior.
validation_basket.continue_if: ETH 4h still trades enough to show the same reclaim thesis structurally.

long: Exit remaining 20% on touch of EMA_55 or loss of VWAP
short: Exit remaining 20% on touch of EMA_55 or regain of VWAP
